summary: Gruber catalogs Zoom's pattern of privacy violations, from secretly installing a hidden web server on Macs to sending user data to Facebook without disclosure. He argues these aren't mistakes but reflect an institutionally cavalier attitude toward privacy. While acknowledging Zoom's video conferencing technology is best-of-breed and exploding in popularity during the pandemic, he contends the company is fundamentally untrustworthy. He recommends using Zoom only on iOS where App Store sandboxing provides protection, and highlights the Mac installer's deceptive preflight behavior as further evidence of bad faith.
key_points: Zoom's iOS app was caught sending analytics data to Facebook even for users without Facebook accounts, and their privacy policy didn't disclose this
This follows Zoom's 2019 scandal of secretly installing a persistent web server on Macs that enabled camera hijacking — which Zoom defended as a feature
Zoom's Mac installer performs the entire installation during the preflight stage, bypassing user review of what gets installed
Gruber recommends using Zoom only on iOS where App Store sandboxing limits what the app can do, or via the web version in a private browser window
Zoom's privacy violations appear systematic and institutional rather than accidental — a pattern of choosing convenience and data collection over user trust
money_quotes: Mistakes happen. Bugs happen. I not only forgive mistakes, I enjoy forgiving mistakes. But Zoom's callous disregard for privacy does not seem to be a mistake.
All of the following can be — and I believe are — true: Zoom is popular, useful, and by their own admission not trustworthy.
This is a company with a history of playing fast and loose with privacy and security.
It betrays Zoom's institutionally cavalier attitude to privacy.
Their privacy policy more or less grants them carte blanche to do whatever the hell they want.
As Zoom itself said about the hidden web server they secretly installed on Macs, it's a feature not a bug.
The Mac version of Zoom is not available through the App Store, which makes me trust it not a bit.
key_insight: Zoom's repeated privacy violations aren't bugs or oversights but reflect a company culture that systematically prioritizes growth and convenience over user trust, making it fundamentally untrustworthy despite its technical excellence.
